---
title: "Your Paper Title"
author: |
  Author One\textsuperscript{\normalsize\orcidlink{0000-0000-0000-0001}}\thanks{Email: \href{mailto:author.one@example.org}{author.one@example.org}. Address: Department, University, City, Country.} ,
  Author Two\textsuperscript{\normalsize\orcidlink{0000-0000-0000-0002}}\thanks{Email: \href{mailto:author.two@example.org}{author.two@example.org}. Address: Department, University, City, Country.} , and
  Author Three\textsuperscript{\normalsize\orcidlink{0000-0000-0000-0003}}\thanks{Email: \href{mailto:author.three@example.org}{author.three@example.org}. Corresponding author. Address: Department, University, City, Country.}
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  We study the cooling of a hot cup of coffee left undisturbed in a
  room at constant ambient temperature. Fitting Newton's law of
  cooling to a sample of repeated measurements yields a cooling
  constant of $k \approx 0.024$ per minute, implying a half-cooling
  time of roughly 29 minutes. The fit is tight, the residuals are
  small, and a single-parameter exponential decay accommodates the
  data over the observed range. Robustness checks vary the
  ambient-temperature assumption and the sampling interval; the
  central estimate is unchanged.

  \bigskip
  \noindent\textbf{Keywords:} Newton's law of cooling, exponential
  decay, nonlinear least squares, coffee

  \noindent\textbf{JEL Classification:} A00, B00, C00
keywords:
  - Newton's law of cooling
  - exponential decay
  - nonlinear least squares
  - coffee
thanks: |
  We thank colleagues for helpful comments and the staff of the
  coffee bar where the measurements were collected. Contact: see
  \href{mailto:author.three@example.org}{author.three@example.org}.
  Replace this acknowledgments block with your own.
bibliography: references.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{amsthm}
        \usepackage{subcaption}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{tabularx}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{sectsty}
        \usepackage{orcidlink}

        \AtBeginEnvironment{tabular}{\onehalfspacing}

        % Inject the italic "Preliminary draft" line between the
        % title and the author block by redefining \title so it
        % appends the subtitle to whatever Pandoc passes in.
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\title}[1]{%
          \gdef\@title{#1\\[0.3cm]%
            {\normalsize\itshape Preliminary draft --- please do not cite without permission}%
          }%
        }
        \makeatother

        % Italic subsection / subsubsection headings, with both
        % sectsty and an explicit \@startsection override so the
        % article-class default \bfseries cannot leak through.
        \subsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \subsubsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\subsection}{\@startsection{subsection}{2}{\z@}%
          {-3.25ex \@plus -1ex \@minus -.2ex}{1.5ex \@plus .2ex}%
          {\normalfont\large\itshape}}
        \renewcommand{\subsubsection}{\@startsection{subsubsection}{3}{\z@}%
          {-3.25ex \@plus -1ex \@minus -.2ex}{1.5ex \@plus .2ex}%
          {\normalfont\large\itshape}}
        \makeatother

        % Period after the section number.
        \makeatletter
        \def\@seccntformat#1{\@ifundefined{#1@cntformat}%
            {\csname the#1\endcsname\quad}%
            {\csname #1@cntformat\endcsname}%
        }
        \def\section@cntformat{\thesection.\quad}
        \def\subsection@cntformat{\thesubsection \quad}
        \makeatother

        \tolerance=1
        \emergencystretch=\maxdimen
        \hyphenpenalty=10000
        \hbadness=10000

        \renewenvironment{quote}
          {\normalsize\list{}{\rightmargin=1.5cm \leftmargin=1.5cm}%
           \item\relax}
          {\endlist}

        % Symmetric space above AND below every display equation.
        % LaTeX's \abovedisplayskip / \belowdisplayskip get scaled
        % by linestretch (doublespacing) and the short-variant
        % skips are auto-selected based on the previous line's
        % length, both of which make pure-\setlength approaches
        % asymmetric in practice. We instead zero all four display
        % skips and wrap \equation in a renew that explicitly emits
        % \vspace*{0.4cm} above and below.
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \setlength{\abovedisplayskip}{0pt}%
          \setlength{\belowdisplayskip}{0pt}%
          \setlength{\abovedisplayshortskip}{0pt}%
          \setlength{\belowdisplayshortskip}{0pt}%
        }
        \let\oldequation=\equation
        \let\endoldequation=\endequation
        \renewenvironment{equation}{%
          \vspace*{0.4cm}\oldequation%
        }{%
          \endoldequation\vspace*{0.4cm}%
        }

        \theoremstyle{plain}
        \newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}[section]
        \newtheorem{proposition}[theorem]{Proposition}
        \newtheorem{lemma}[theorem]{Lemma}
        \theoremstyle{definition}
        \newtheorem{definition}[theorem]{Definition}
        \newtheorem{assumption}[theorem]{Assumption}
---

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

A cup of coffee left on a desk cools toward room temperature.
Empirically the cooling looks roughly exponential: the hotter the
coffee is at a given moment, the faster it loses heat to its
surroundings. Newton's law of cooling provides a one-parameter
description of this behaviour. This paper fits the law to a small
sample of coffee-cup temperature measurements and reports the
implied cooling constant.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section
@sec-theory states Newton's law and derives the testable
implication. Section @sec-data describes the measurement protocol
and the sample. Section @sec-method explains the estimator.
Section @sec-results reports the fit. Sections @sec-additional
and @sec-robust discuss extensions and robustness. Section
@sec-conc concludes.

# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

Let $T(t)$ denote the temperature of the coffee at time $t$ and
let $T_\infty$ denote the (constant) ambient temperature of the
room. Newton's law of cooling asserts that the instantaneous rate
of cooling is proportional to the temperature difference between
the coffee and its surroundings,

$$
\frac{dT(t)}{dt} = -k\, \bigl(T(t) - T_\infty\bigr),
$$ {#eq-newton}

with cooling constant $k > 0$. Integrating @eq-newton subject to
the initial condition $T(0) = T_0$ yields the closed-form
solution

$$
T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)\, e^{-k t}.
$$ {#eq-solution}

## Testable Predictions {#sec-theory-predictions}

@eq-solution implies that the logarithm of the standardised
temperature difference is linear in time,

$$
\log\!\bigl(T(t) - T_\infty\bigr) = \log(T_0 - T_\infty) - k\, t,
$$ {#eq-log}

which provides the empirical specification estimated below.

::: {.proposition}
Under @eq-newton, the half-cooling time of the coffee --- the
time required for the gap $T(t) - T_\infty$ to halve --- is
$t_{1/2} = \log 2 / k$ and is independent of the initial
temperature $T_0$.
:::

# Data {#sec-data}

## Measurement Protocol {#sec-data-protocol}

We prepared a single cup of brewed coffee in a standard ceramic
mug and recorded its temperature with a digital thermocouple at
one-minute intervals over 60 minutes. The room temperature was
held at $T_\infty = 22^\circ$C throughout. We repeated the
protocol on five separate days; the analysis below uses the
average across the five runs.

## Variable Definitions {#sec-data-vars}

The dependent variable is the temperature reading $T_t$ in
degrees Celsius at minute $t \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 60\}$. Full
variable definitions appear in Appendix @sec-app-vardesc.

## Summary Statistics {#sec-data-summary}

Table @tbl-summary reports the mean and standard deviation of
the temperature across the five runs, at selected minutes.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics for the coffee-cup cooling sample."
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
# knitr::kable(your_data_frame, booktabs = TRUE)
```

# Methodology {#sec-method}

Taking logs of @eq-solution and subtracting $T_\infty$ yields a
specification linear in $k$. Following @example2025, we estimate
$k$ by nonlinear least squares on the level form,

$$
\widehat{k} = \arg\min_{k}\, \sum_{t=0}^{60} \Bigl[T_t - T_\infty - (T_0 - T_\infty)\, e^{-k t}\Bigr]^{2}.
$$ {#eq-nls}

Standard errors are obtained from the asymptotic covariance of
the NLS estimator and reported in parentheses below.

# Main Results {#sec-results}

## Baseline Estimate {#sec-results-baseline}

The point estimate is $\widehat{k} = 0.024$ per minute (standard
error 0.002), implying a half-cooling time of $t_{1/2} =
\log 2 / 0.024 \approx 29$ minutes. The mean squared residual is
$0.6^\circ$C, suggesting that a one-parameter exponential captures
the data well.

## Heterogeneity {#sec-results-hetero}

Splitting the sample into the first and last 30 minutes yields
nearly identical $\widehat{k}$ values, with overlapping confidence
intervals. The cooling-constant interpretation is therefore
stable across the observation window.

## Mechanisms {#sec-results-mechanism}

The estimated $\widehat{k}$ depends on heat-transfer coefficients
at the mug-air interface (convection) and at the coffee-mug
interface (conduction). We do not separately identify these
channels but discuss them informally in Appendix
@sec-app-derivations.

# Additional Analyses {#sec-additional}

Allowing $T_\infty$ to vary across runs gives an alternative
estimator that is more robust to small ambient-temperature
fluctuations. The point estimate of $k$ is unchanged at the
third decimal place.

# Robustness {#sec-robust}

The baseline survives standard robustness checks: trimming the
first three minutes (when the coffee is being stirred), using
the median across runs rather than the mean, and re-fitting on
the log-linearised specification of @eq-log. Detailed results
appear in the Internet Appendix.

# Conclusion {#sec-conc}

A single cooling constant $\widehat{k} \approx 0.024$ per minute
describes the cooling of a hot coffee cup in a constant-temperature
room well. The fit is consistent with the predictions of Newton's
law and with prior estimates reported in the literature.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

```{=tex}
\newpage
\appendix
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
\renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{section}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{figure}}
\renewcommand{\theequation}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{equation}}
```

# Variable Definitions {#sec-app-vardesc}

A formal table of every variable used in @sec-data: the
temperature reading $T_t$ (degrees Celsius, recorded by a
digital thermocouple), the time index $t$ (minutes since
brewing), the ambient temperature $T_\infty$ (degrees Celsius,
measured at the start of each run), and the initial temperature
$T_0$ (degrees Celsius, the first reading of each run).

# Auxiliary Derivations {#sec-app-derivations}

The closed-form solution @eq-solution follows from separation of
variables applied to @eq-newton.

::: {.proof}
Rearranging @eq-newton gives $dT / (T - T_\infty) = -k\, dt$.
Integrating both sides between $0$ and $t$ yields
$\log(T(t) - T_\infty) - \log(T_0 - T_\infty) = -k\, t$, and
exponentiating produces @eq-solution. $\square$
:::
